$comment: Glossary terms used in word_formation explanations not yet in data/glossary.json. Project author reviews and merges. Checked against glossary.json v4 (term 'lexicalised' already present, reused).

alterato.short: An altered form of a noun made by adding an evaluative suffix: gattino, librone, tempaccio. It resizes or colours the word without changing what it is.
alterato.long: The alterati are the evaluative suffixes of Italian. They take a base noun and add feeling or size while keeping the same referent: a gattino is still a gatto, a librone is still a libro. Three families do the work: the diminutivo (small/affectionate), the accrescitivo (big), and the peggiorativo (bad quality). Not every noun takes every suffix, and some altered-looking words are falsi alterati where the base meaning has gone.
alterato.needed_for: The four-beat term in nearly every diminutive, augmentative and pejorative explanation.

diminutivo.short: A suffix (-ino, -etto, -ello, -uccio) making something small and often affectionate: tavolino, sorellina, momentino.
diminutivo.long: The diminutivo shrinks a noun and frequently warms it: sorellina is 'dear little sister', affection as much as size. Suffix choice is partly fixed by usage (momentino, not momentetto), and several diminutives have lexicalised into their own words (cucchiaino = teaspoon). Produce the common attested forms; recognise the rest.
diminutivo.needed_for: Named and reused across the diminutives leaf explanations.

accrescitivo.short: The suffix -one making something big: librone, portone. It can shift a feminine base to masculine (la porta > il portone).
accrescitivo.long: The accrescitivo -one enlarges a noun. Its trap is gender: many feminine bases turn masculine under -one (la porta > il portone; la palla > il pallone), so the article is part of the answer. Keep it distinct from the diminutivo (small) and the peggiorativo (bad).
accrescitivo.needed_for: Named in the augmentative explanations, especially the gender-shift items.

peggiorativo.short: A suffix (-accio, -accia) marking bad quality, not size: tempaccio (foul weather), parolaccia (swear word).
peggiorativo.long: The peggiorativo passes a negative judgement on quality: tempaccio is bad weather, ragazzaccio a badly-behaved boy. The common error is to read -accio as size (like the accrescitivo -one); it is about badness, so tempaccio is foul, not big.
peggiorativo.needed_for: Named and reused across the pejorative explanations.

falso alterato.short: A word that only looks altered: cavalletto is an easel, not a small cavallo; focaccia is a bread, not a bad foca.
falso alterato.long: A falso alterato has the shape of an alterato (base + evaluative suffix) but not the meaning: the base word has vanished from the sense. Cavalletto (easel), mattino (morning), burrone (ravine) and tacchino (turkey) look altered but are independent words. The test is whether the base meaning survives; if it does not, the word is a falso alterato.
falso alterato.needed_for: The core term of the falsi leaf; named in every falsi explanation.

prefisso intensivo.short: A prefix (stra-, arci-, super-, ultra-) that intensifies an adjective: strapieno, arcistufo, superveloce.
prefisso intensivo.long: The intensifying prefixes raise an adjective to its top degree, acting like an absolute superlative built without -issimo (strapieno is close to pienissimo). They attach to adjectives, not freely to nouns, and need no separate 'molto'. The superlative frame they express belongs to the comparison topic.
prefisso intensivo.needed_for: Named across the intensifiers leaf explanations.
